schools.summary: Gill: Bashan was a fine country for pasturage and famous for its oaks, the same called Batanea.
Henry: another brave country delivered into the hand of Israel.

themes: Og the king of Bashan came out against us... to battle at Edrei
another aggressor walking into judgment
the victories coming swiftly as God went before

schools.summary: Gill: this was spoken when Og was marching with all his forces against Israel.
Keil & Delitzsch: Bashan was named from its soft, level soil.

themes: Fear him not: for I will deliver him... into thy hand
the word arming Israel against a giant
dread turned to certainty of triumph

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD delivered Og king of Bashan, as well as Sihon king of Heshbon; none of his people was left remaining.

themes: the LORD our God delivered into our hands Og also... we smote him until none was left
the giant king overthrown by God
no might of man standing against him

schools.summary: Gill: they took not only Edrei and Ashteroth his capital but all the rest in his kingdom.
Clarke: 'the region of Argob' is literally the 'cord' or measured tract of Argob.

themes: there was not a city which we took not from them, threescore cities, all the region of Argob
the whole kingdom delivered up
God giving the land wholly

schools.summary: Gill: though the cities were fenced with high walls, gates, and bars, it hindered not their falling into Israel's hands; this might encourage them against the fears raised by the spies.

themes: All these cities were fenced with high walls, gates, and bars
the strong cities falling without hindrance
no fortification standing against God

schools.summary: Gill: it was the inhabitants, not the cities, that were utterly destroyed.
Ellicott: 'utterly destroyed' means devoted, made cherem, as before (Deut. 2:34).

themes: we utterly destroyed them, as we did unto Sihon... of every city
the cherem of God's judgment, not cruelty
the same righteous sentence on a doomed kingdom (Gen. 15:16)

schools.summary: Gill: the oxen, sheep, camels, asses, gold, silver, household furniture, stores of corn, and all their substance were taken as a prey for Israel.

themes: all the cattle, and the spoil of the cities, we took for a prey to ourselves
the measured justice of the cherem
God enriching his people out of the conquest

schools.summary: Gill: this was taken out of the hands of the two kings, Sihon of Heshbon and Og of Bashan.
Wesley: 'on this side Jordan' was so when Moses wrote, but called 'beyond Jordan' after Israel crossed.

themes: we took... the land... from the river of Arnon unto mount Hermon
the whole east-Jordan conquest
the first inheritance secured by God's victories

schools.summary: Gill: Sirion was the name the Sidonians gave it, well known to Tyre and Sidon, whence snow was brought in summer.
Clarke: takes the verse to be an explanatory note on the various names.

themes: Which Hermon the Sidonians call Sirion; and the Amorites call it Shenir
the famed mountain marking the northern bound
the record fixed with care

schools.summary: Gill: there was a plain by Medeba, and Heshbon and her cities were in a plain, given to Reuben.
Ellicott: Salchah is the present large town Salkhad, east of Bashan.

themes: All the cities of the plain, and all Gilead, and all Bashan, unto Salchah and Edrei
the whole kingdom of Og possessed
the giant king's realm secured entire

schools.summary: Gill: Og was the only one left of the race of giants, his iron bedstead nine cubits long preserved as a memorial of his bulk and strength.

themes: only Og king of Bashan remained of the remnant of giants; behold, his bedstead was a bedstead of iron
the last of the giants overthrown
the mightiest falling before God, that the faithful not fear

schools.summary: Gill: the land was possessed and still remained in their hand.
Keil & Delitzsch: from Aroer by Arnon and half Gilead given to the Reubenites and Gadites.

themes: from Aroer... and half mount Gilead... gave I unto the Reubenites and to the Gadites
the conquered land distributed
God's gift orderly parcelled among his people

schools.summary: Gill: the other half of the mount with its cities was given to half Manasseh.
Keil & Delitzsch: all Bashan, the land of giants, given to the half tribe of Manasseh.

themes: the rest of Gilead, and all Bashan, being the kingdom of Og, gave I unto the half tribe of Manasseh
the land of giants become Israel's inheritance
the strongholds of the mighty overturned

schools.summary: Gill: Jair took all the country of Argob, or Trachonitis, the small towns of Gilead.
Ellicott: the words 'unto this day' seem to point to a later hand, as of Joshua, describing the completion.

themes: Jair... took all the country of Argob... and called them after his own name, Bashan-havoth-jair
the land settled by its conqueror
a lasting memorial of the inheritance secured

schools.summary: Gill: this was not to Machir personally, who cannot be thought living then, but to his posterity, the Machirites.
Keil & Delitzsch: Machir received Gilead (Num. 32:40).

themes: And I gave Gilead unto Machir
the portion given to his posterity
the inheritance passing to the new generation

schools.summary: Gill: the gift to the tribes of Reuben and Gad.
Keil & Delitzsch: from Gilead to Arnon, and to the Jabbok, the border of the children of Ammon.

themes: unto the Reubenites and unto the Gadites I gave from Gilead even unto the river Arnon... unto the river Jabbok
the inheritance bounded with order
the border God set for Ammon honoured

schools.summary: Gill: the plain by Jordan, the plains of Moab on its side, together with the river.
Wesley: the Salt Sea was, before the dreadful conflagration, a goodly plain.

themes: The plain also, and Jordan, and the coast thereof, from Chinnereth even unto the... salt sea
the bounds marked to the Jordan valley
the Salt Sea recalling the judgment on Sodom

schools.summary: Gill: this was spoken not to all Israel but to Reuben, Gad, and half Manasseh. Calvin: the agreement that the two tribes and a half should not enjoy their possession unless they helped their brethren win theirs.

themes: ye shall pass over armed before your brethren the children of Israel
the settled bearing their part in the war
not living to themselves but for all (Gal. 6:2)

schools.summary: Gill: the wives, little ones, and cattle were to be left behind, abiding in the cities God had given them.

themes: your wives, and your little ones, and your cattle... shall abide in your cities
the families left in God's keeping
their brethren's need before their own ease

schools.summary: Gill: the land of Canaan is called a rest, typical of the rest that remains for the people of God; then each might return to his own possession.

themes: Until the LORD have given rest unto your brethren, as well as unto you
Canaan a type of the rest that remains
the faithful entering the rest together (Heb. 4:9)

schools.summary: Gill: this charge was given after the conquest of the two kings, and after Moses knew he should die and Joshua succeed him.
Henry: the encouragement Moses gave to Joshua, his successor.

themes: Thine eyes have seen all that the LORD... hath done unto these two kings: so shall the LORD do unto all the kingdoms
the past victory a pledge of those to come
God's former works the warrant of faith (1 Sam. 17:37)

schools.summary: Gill: they were not to fear the numbers, strength, courage, or gigantic stature of the inhabitants, nor their walled cities.
Keil & Delitzsch: the reason — Jehovah himself would fight for them.

themes: Ye shall not fear them: for the LORD your God he shall fight for you
God's own presence the ground of courage
fear cast out, if God be for us (Rom. 8:31)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses, told he should die and Joshua succeed him, and seeing the conquest of the two kings as the earnest of the promise, was desirous to live to see the work completed, and sought the Lord by prayer.

themes: And I besought the LORD at that time
Moses' earnest prayer to enter the land
desire poured out, the answer left to God

schools.summary: Gill: God had begun to show his greatness in subduing the two kings; Moses had seen his power in Egypt, at the Red sea, and in the wilderness, but this was the beginning of his power against the Canaanites; none can do according to his works.

themes: what God is there in heaven or in earth, that can do according to thy works
worship woven into Moses' prayer
the LORD peerless among the gods (Exod. 15:11)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was desirous of going over Jordan to see the land flowing with milk and honey, a most excellent land.
Wesley: he supposed God's threatening might admit of being reversed by prayer.

themes: let me go over, and see the good land that is beyond Jordan
a holy longing for the place of blessing
the boldness of faith, yet God's decree to stand

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD was wroth not for this prayer but on account of Moses and Aaron not sanctifying him at the waters of Meribah, and the unadvised words that dropped through Israel's provocation.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD would not hear him, and bade him speak no more of the matter.

themes: the LORD was wroth with me for your sakes... Let it suffice thee; speak no more unto me of this matter
the prayer denied, the Meribah fault standing
God saying no even to a holy plea

schools.summary: Gill: Pisgah was the highest eminence of Mount Nebo, a proper place to take a prospect.
Keil & Delitzsch: he was bidden to behold the land on every side, but not to go over Jordan.

themes: behold it with thine eyes: for thou shalt not go over this Jordan
Moses shown the land but not to enter
the law sees the rest yet brings not in (Heb. 4:8)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses was to charge Joshua to take care of the people, encourage him against fear, and strengthen him with the promises of God's presence and help.
Keil & Delitzsch: Joshua should go over before this people and cause them to inherit the land.

themes: charge Joshua, and encourage him, and strengthen him: for he shall go over before this people
God's work continuing beyond Moses
Joshua a figure of the true Joshua who brings in

schools.summary: Gill: this was in the plains of Moab, over against a temple of Baal-peor upon a mountain; this is the valley where Moses was buried.

themes: So we abode in the valley over against Beth-peor
the last encampment and place of Moses' grave
the people poised by Jordan to hear the law
